aoi.aoi_type: named producing uranium mine / mineral deposit
aoi.geometry_confidence: point-level only; no claim or lease polygon supplied

overall_result.uranium_potential: confirmed very high at the known mine/deposit scale
overall_result.extension_potential: moderate to high along documented near-mine trends where geophysics, structure, alteration, and drilling support follow-up
overall_result.greenfield_surface_geochemistry_signal_n: weak in the checked local lake-water data within 25 km
overall_result.confidence: high for known deposit presence and production; moderate for near-mine extension potential; low for any new target outside documented drill/geophysics corridors

evidence_lanes.finding: SMDI record 1856 identifies Cigar Lake Mine as a production uranium deposit with associated Co, Cu, Pb, Ni, and Zn.
evidence_lanes.evidence_grade: A for occurrence identity and status; not used as NI 43-101 reserve evidence

evidence_lanes.finding: Cigar Lake is located at the Athabasca Group/Wollaston Group unconformity, with graphitic pelitic basement, east-west structural control, basement-high control, and strong clay alteration.
The deposit is explicitly described as an unconformity-related uranium deposit and matches the expected redox/unconformity, graphitic fault, clay alteration, and uraninite-pitchblende model elements.

Technical-report and SMDI evidence supports U with Ni, Co, Cu, Pb, Zn, Mo, As associations at the deposit. Local lake-water samples within 25 km show weak U values, which are not decisive for this deep covered deposit.
evidence_lanes.evidence_grade: A for deposit geochemistry; B/C for local lake-water screening

evidence_lanes.finding: The deposit was discovered by drilling geophysical anomalies, and EM/resistivity methods remain central for near-mine exploration.
Cameco reports 2025 proven and probable reserves at 479.0 thousand tonnes grading 16.33 percent U3O8, containing 172.4 million pounds U3O8 on a 100 percent basis.
evidence_lanes.evidence_grade: A for public company NI 43-101 style reporting; no independent audit performed in this screen

evidence_lanes.finding: Cigar Lake is operating under a CNSC licence expiring June 30, 2031, and ore is processed at McClean Lake. MCP did not calculate infrastructure distances.
evidence_lanes.evidence_grade: A for operating and licence status; D for uncalculated infrastructure distance
evidence_lanes.confidence: high for status, low for distance metrics

assumptions: The requested AOI is the Cigar Lake mine/deposit area, not the full Waterbury property, all surrounding claims, or a new MARS disposition.
The SMDI point and approximate lat/lon are adequate for screening only, not for distance, area, tenure, or permitting conclusions.
Public official and company sources are sufficient for a first-pass screening memo; no proprietary drillhole database or mine model was accessed.
Cameco reserve and production data are treated as current to the stated effective date of December 31, 2025.
data_gaps: No authoritative lease, claim, or mine-boundary polygon was loaded.
No live Saskatchewan GeoAtlas, SMDI API, SMAD, CDoGS, or Open Canada CKAN data were downloaded through MCP.
No raw drillhole collar, assay, alteration, geotechnical, or geophysical grids were compiled.
The CNSC licence and licence conditions handbook were not obtained.
Local lake-water geochemistry was not QA/QC audited and has limited relevance to a deep, covered deposit.
recommended_next_steps: Resolve authoritative AOI geometry from SMDI, GeoAtlas, MARS, and mine-lease records before spatial calculations.
Download and normalize SMDI 1856, related SMAD assessment reports, and current Cameco reserve/resource PDFs.
Compile drill collars, assays, alteration, EM, resistivity, and structure into a 3D or section-based near-mine targeting model.
Separate known-mine reserve/resource review from greenfield target ranking around Cigar East, CLEXT, and surrounding Waterbury claim trends.
Review CNSC licence conditions, regulatory actions, IEMP data, and environmental monitoring obligations before any work-program recommendation.
Use projected CRS analysis for infrastructure, community, environmental, and tenure distances.
